Your problem statement is classic CDC (Change Data Capture):

- source files contain row-level change events
- each event includes an operation flag (insert/update/delete)
- and a timestamp/sequence that defines the order of changes
- you need to apply those events to keep a target table current

Memory Hook: "Text me the default"
The answer is dbutils.widgets.**text**() — and the parameter order is: Name → Default → Label

dbutils.widgets.text("department", "all", "Department")

dbutils.widgets.text(name, defaultValue, label)

name — the key you use to retrieve it with .get("department")
defaultValue — what it falls back to if nothing is passed at runtime ("all")
label — just the display label shown in the notebook UI widget panel

The label is optional in practice, but the exam shows it with all three. The label is purely cosmetic — it doesn't affect .get() at all.
So the mental model is: one for code, one for fallback, one for humans.

text
Free-form input, single value

dropdown
Pick one from a fixed list

combobox
Pick from list or type your own

multiselect
Pick one or more from a list
assert is a statement, not a function.

The rule: Streaming DataFrames don't support operations that require ordering across the entire dataset — because the data is infinite and never "done."

A — simple SELECT ✅ fine
B — GROUP BY with count ✅ fine (supported via windowing/watermarks)
C — adding a column ✅ fine
D — WHERE filter ✅ fine
E — ORDER BY ❌ not supported — you can't sort a stream globally

Memory hook:

"Lakehouse = yes to everything."

Any time an exam question asks why Lakehouse replaces both lakes and warehouses, and "all of the above" is an option — it's almost certainly E. The whole point of Lakehouse is that it's not one trick, it's the complete package.
The trap is picking the one that sounds most distinctive (streaming+batch feels like the "special" Lakehouse feature), but the real answer is that no single feature replaces two systems — the combination does.

Memory hook for E:

"Small results phone home (control plane), big results stay in your house (customer storage)."

Notebook results are split — the UI-rendered portion in the control plane, overflow/larger results in your own storage. Neither alone is the complete answer.

Memory hook:
"No trigger = hair trigger — 500ms."

500 milliseconds is half a second — Spark Structured Streaming is designed to be near real-time by default. It's not waiting around for 5 minutes.
Memory hook: Production = professional/cost-conscious → shuts everything down when done. Development = your laptop stays on while you're working.

Yeah, 100% — and this is a good conceptual question, not just syntax.
The key insight you missed:
Spark is a distributed execution engine. Breakpoints (D) and variable watchers (A) are single-process debugger concepts — they don't work when your code is running across a cluster of executors. There's no single place to "pause and inspect."
Why B is the only practical answer:

df.printSchema() — shows column names and types
print(df.dtypes) — returns a list of (column, type) tuples
These run on the driver, before the distributed job fans out

Memory hook: "Spark doesn't pause — it prints"
Any exam question about debugging Spark jobs → think logging and schema inspection, not IDE-style debugging tools. Breakpoints, variable watchers, step-through debuggers = single machine concepts that don't translate to distributed systems.
Quick elimination for similar questions:

Variable watcher → ❌ single process only
Breakpoint → ❌ distributed, nothing to pause
Spark UI → ❌ good for performance/DAG, not runtime data types
Print/log schema → ✅ always works, runs on driver

This one's worth locking in because the underlying concept (distributed = no breakpoints) will show up in other forms on the exam.

Memory hook: "Spark tells you what happened in the logs, not in a debugger."

Memory hook: "The format is the protocol, not the database"
JDBC is the protocol. SQLite/MySQL/Postgres are the databases. You tell Spark the protocol ("jdbc"), and the URL tells it which database. Same pattern every time regardless of the database.
Memory hook: "fail = failOnNewColumn, add = addNewColumns, rescue = rescue"

Memory hook: "Star counts all, column counts real"
COUNT(*) = every row. COUNT(col) = only rows where col exists (not null).
Burn in the NULL comparison rule while you're here:

You can NEVER compare NULL with =, !=, <>, >, < — always returns UNKNOWN, never TRUE
Only valid NULL checks: IS NULL and IS NOT NULL. Full stop.
Watch for "select two" questions on the exam — they'll often pair an obvious correct answer (A) with a subtle one (D) that tests whether you know the implicit behavior of a function. When you see select-two, always ask yourself if there's a function-based answer hiding alongside the explicit filter answer.

Memory hook: "global_temp is short, like a temp view should be"
The word temporary gets abbreviated to temp everywhere in Databricks — TEMP VIEW, global_temp schema. If you're ever tempted to spell it out fully, don't.

Memory hook: "Open door for outsiders, Databricks door for insiders"
No Databricks account → Open Sharing (they get a credential file to connect from any compatible tool)
Has Databricks → Databricks-to-Databricks sharing
The credential file detail is worth knowing too — Open Sharing works by giving the recipient a bearer token/credential file they plug into Tableau or whatever BI tool they use. No Databricks login required.

The platform constraint worth knowing:
Databricks only allows one metastore per region per account. So one-per-workspace (E) isn't even possible — metastores are account-scoped, not workspace-scoped.
How to organize within a region:
Use catalogs to separate business units, teams, or environments inside a regional metastore. That's what A describes — it's good practice within a region, just doesn't solve the multi-region problem.
Memory hook: "One metastore per region, one catalog per team"
Region → metastore. Business unit → catalog. Those are the two org levels to keep straight.
